preamble: The voiced retroflex lateral approximant [ɭ] shifts to the alveolar trill [r] through a historical merger documented across South Dravidian languages. This represents a major phonological simplification from the Proto-Dravidian three-way liquid distinction (l/ḷ/r) to a two-way system in modern Kannada and related languages, where the retroflex and alveolar liquids merged.

examples.note: Complete merger: historical retroflex lateral merged with alveolar trill before 10th century. Proto-Dravidian *aḷ-a 'price' merged with *ar-a forms in modern Kannada.
Modern Kannada shows merged central approximant and retroflex lateral as alveolar [r], marking completion of the liquids merger.
Tamil maintained more conservative three-way contrast, but Sri Lankan Tamil shows partial merger toward /r/.
Dialectal variation: traditional three-way distinction remains, but some dialects show merger.
Malayalam preserves three-way liquid system formally, but casual speech shows merger tendencies.
